did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning architectures (MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom model) to classify skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanoma, and melanocytic nevi) using the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and evaluation of deep learning architectures (MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom model) for skin lesion classification using the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets. It includes specific methodology sections (Proposed Classification System, Datasets, Image Augmentation), results with quantitative metrics (Test Accuracy, AUC, F1 Score), and a comparison of model performances, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification using Deep Learning Architectures
journal: 2020 3rd International Conference on Communication System, Computing and IT Applications (CSCITA)
brief_summary: This study evaluates deep learning architectures, specifically MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom-designed model, for classifying six types of skin lesions: basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanoma, and melanocytic nevi. The researchers utilized the PH2 and HAM10000 dermoscopic image datasets, applying data augmentation techniques to balance class distributions and improve model robustness. Performance was assessed using test accuracy, Area Under the Curve (AUC), and F1 score metrics across both augmented and non-augmented conditions. The custom model achieved the highest accuracy of 97.25% on the augmented PH2 dataset, while MobileNet and the custom model performed comparably on the HAM10000 dataset with accuracies around 82%. The results indicate that the proposed custom architecture and MobileNet are effective for skin lesion classification, whereas VGG-16 showed lower performance. The authors conclude that these models can assist dermatologists in early detection and reduce unnecessary medical anxiety.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in the HAM10000 dataset (514 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation). Mentioned in the abstract as a class that can become malignant.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset (1099 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation). Referred to as 'benign keratosis' in the abstract.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset (115 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation). Mentioned in the abstract.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset (142 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation). Mentioned in the abstract.
Studied in both PH2 (40 images without augmentation, 2000 with augmentation) and HAM10000 (1113 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation) datasets. Classified as malignant.

Studied in both PH2 (80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi without augmentation; 2000 non-melanoma total with augmentation) and HAM10000 (6705 images without augmentation, 4415 with augmentation) datasets. In PH2, common and atypical nevi were grouped as 'non-melanoma'.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset (327 images without augmentation, 5000 with augmentation). Mentioned in the abstract as a type that can become malignant.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanoma, and melanocytic nevi) and binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma labels.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'Skin Lesion Classification' using deep learning architectures (MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom model). The models predict discrete classes from the PH2 dataset (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and the HAM10000 dataset (seven specific lesion types: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Test Accuracy, AUC, and F1 Score.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset containing 200 lesion images with a maximum resolution of 768 × 560 pixels. It includes RGB color images evaluated by a dermatologist. The dataset was augmented using the Augmentor library to create balanced classes.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (MobileNet, VGG-16, Custom Model) after augmentation to 3200 images (1600 melanoma, 1600 non-melanoma).
use.validation: Used for validation with 400 labeled images (200 melanoma, 200 non-melanoma).
use.testing: Used for testing with 400 labeled images (200 melanoma, 200 non-melanoma).
use.other: Original un-augmented dataset consisted of 80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, and 40 melanoma images.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The dataset consists of 10,015 labeled dermoscopic images which were pathologically verified by dermatologists. It was augmented using the Augmentor library to balance class distributions.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models after augmentation to 34,415 images (5000 per class for akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, vasc; 4415 for nv).
use.validation: Used for validation with 552 labeled images.
use.testing: Used for testing with 551 labeled images.
use.other: Original un-augmented dataset consisted of 151 bcc, 175 bkl, 39 df, 230 mel, 4415 nv, and 64 vasc images (Note: Text states 10015 total but lists class sums that differ slightly in Table IV vs text description; Table IV lists 151, 175, 440, 39, 230, 4415, 64 totaling 5515 non-nv classes + 4415 nv = 9930? Text says 10015. The counts below reflect the explicit per-class numbers provided in the text for the original dataset: 327 akiec, 514 bcc, 1099 bkl, 115 df, 1113 mel, 6705 nv, 142 vasc).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

PH2 Dataset (Without Augmentation).notes: The PH2 dataset without augmentation consisted of 200 images (80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanoma). Common and atypical nevi were combined as non-melanoma. The dataset was split into 3200 training, 400 validation, and 400 testing images after augmentation, but these specific results are for the non-augmented public dataset evaluation.

PH2 Dataset (With Augmentation).notes: The PH2 dataset was augmented using Augmentor to create a balanced dataset of 4000 labeled images (2000 Non-Melanoma, 2000 Melanoma). The Custom Model achieved the highest accuracy of 97.25% with augmentation.

HAM10000 Dataset (Without Augmentation).notes: The HAM10000 dataset without augmentation consisted of 10,015 labeled dermoscopic images. The Custom Model achieved the highest accuracy of 83.152% without augmentation.

HAM10000 Dataset (With Augmentation).notes: The HAM10000 dataset was augmented using Augmentor to balance classes, resulting in 34,415 labeled images. The authors note that data augmentation had a detrimental effect on model performance for this dataset compared to the non-augmented results.

architecture_used.main_model: Three architectures were used: MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom-designed CNN model.
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNet and VGG-16 were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. The custom model was trained from scratch.

training_details.validation_strategy: The model with the minimum validation loss during training was selected (saved via Keras callback).

classification_or_output_head.HAM10000: Dense layer with 7 neurons (for 7 classes: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
classification_or_output_head.PH2: Dense layer with 2 neurons (for 2 classes: melanoma, non-melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using Augmentor library (rotation, flipping, cropping, elastic distortion, shearing, skewing) to balance class distributions
Image resizing to 224 × 224 × 3 RGB input size for MobileNet, VGG-16, and Custom Model
Manual segmentation of skin lesions (mentioned as a parameter evaluated by dermatologists in PH2 dataset)
Combining common nevi and atypical nevi into a single 'non-melanoma' class for PH2 dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to augment images for both PH2 and HAM10000 datasets to balance class distributions. Operations included rotation, flipping (left/right, top/bottom), cropping, elastic distortion, shearing, and skewing. Applied in a stochastic manner according to user-defined probability values. For PH2, augmented to 2000 images per class (4000 total). For HAM10000, augmented to 5000 images per class for most classes (34415 total).

Data augmentation was specifically employed to address class imbalance in the original datasets. PH2 was balanced to 2000 Non-Melanoma and 2000 Melanoma images. HAM10000 was balanced to 5000 images for Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Pigmented Benign Keratoses, Dermatoﬁbroma, Melanoma, and Vascular classes, while Nevus remained at 4415 images.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning models (MobileNet, VGG-16, and a custom CNN) can classify different types of skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanoma, and melanocytic nevi) with an accuracy above 80%.
The custom-designed model achieved the highest test accuracy of 97.25% on the augmented PH2 dataset, outperforming MobileNet (90%) and VGG-16 (50%).

On the HAM10000 dataset, MobileNet achieved the highest test accuracy of 82% without augmentation and 82% with augmentation, while the custom model achieved 83.152% without augmentation and 80.61% with augmentation.

Data augmentation had a detrimental effect on model performance for the HAM10000 dataset, as accuracies decreased or remained similar compared to non-augmented results, whereas it improved performance for the PH2 dataset.

The study concludes that these deep learning architectures can assist dermatologists in early detection of skin cancer and help avoid unnecessary medical anxiety or false alarms by providing accurate pre-diagnosis support.

The authors note that results from the small PH2 dataset without augmentation cannot be generalized well due to the limited number of samples, highlighting a limitation in generalizability for smaller datasets.
important_remarks: The PH2 dataset is extremely small (200 images), and the authors explicitly state that results from this dataset cannot be generalized well due to the limited number of samples.

Data augmentation had a detrimental effect on model performance for the HAM10000 dataset, with accuracies decreasing or remaining similar compared to non-augmented results, contrary to improvements seen in the PH2 dataset.

The HAM10000 dataset used in this study is described as 'manually augmented' in the text, yet the authors also claim to have augmented it further using the Augmentor library, creating potential confusion regarding the data provenance and preprocessing steps.

The HAM10000 dataset contains significant class imbalance in its original form (e.g., 6705 Nevus vs. 115 Dermatofibroma), which the authors attempted to mitigate via augmentation, potentially introducing synthetic bias.
The study relies on publicly available datasets (PH2 and HAM10000) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical applicability.

The paper acknowledges that dermoscopic images often require specific magnification (20x) via tools like the Tubinger Mole Analyser, which limits the generalizability of the model to images captured with different devices or magnifications.

The custom model was trained from scratch, while MobileNet and VGG-16 were pre-trained on ImageNet; the performance gap between the custom model and pre-trained models on HAM10000 is relatively small, questioning the necessity of the custom architecture.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the differences in performance between the models, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy, AUC, and F1 score.
